error.signature: JWT validation bypassed by changing algorithm to 'none' in token header

verdict.summary: RFC 7519 defines 'none' as a valid JWT algorithm (unsecured JWS). If the server doesn't explicitly enforce the expected algorithm, an attacker can forge tokens by setting alg:none and removing the signature. Many JWT libraries accepted this by default.

dead_ends.action: Validate JWT without specifying expected algorithms
dead_ends.why_fails: Without an explicit algorithm whitelist, libraries may accept alg:none tokens. The signature verification is skipped entirely for unsigned tokens.

dead_ends.action: Check the alg field from the JWT header to decide verification method
dead_ends.why_fails: Attacker controls the JWT header. Using attacker-supplied alg means they choose the verification algorithm. Classic confused-deputy attack.

workarounds.action: Always specify allowed algorithms explicitly in jwt.decode()
workarounds.how: jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=['HS256'])  # NEVER omit algorithms parameter

workarounds.action: Use asymmetric algorithms (RS256/ES256) and separate signing/verification keys
workarounds.how: RS256 with private key for signing, public key for verification. Even if alg is changed to HS256, the public key won't produce valid HMAC.

workarounds.action: Reject tokens with alg:none at the middleware level
workarounds.how: header = jwt.get_unverified_header(token); if header['alg'] == 'none': raise SecurityError()
